JUSTIFICATION FOR OTHER THAN FULL AND OPEN COMPETITION
HUBZone Sole Source Award

1. Agency: Department of Veterans Affairs, Network Contracting Office 4, Pittsburgh, PA
2. Contracting Activity: Veterans Health Administration, VA Pittsburgh Healthcare System
3. Description of Action: Sole source award to a HUBZone small business concern for biomedical equipment preventive maintenance and repair services.
4. Authority: FAR 6.302-5(b)(5) – Set-asides for HUBZone small business concerns authorized under FAR 19.1306.

5. Basis for Sole Source Justification:

Pursuant to FAR 19.1306(a), a sole source HUBZone award may be made when the contracting officer does not have a reasonable expectation that offers would be received from two or more HUBZone small business concerns. Market research, including review of SAM.gov registrations, SBA's HUBZone certification database, and prior procurement history, identified MedServ Solutions, Inc. (DUNS: 078451239, CAGE: 8JKP2), located at 2490 Sidney Street, Suite 300, Pittsburgh, PA 15203, as the only certified HUBZone small business concern in the local area with the requisite OEM certifications and trained personnel to service the specific biomedical equipment models currently installed at VA Pittsburgh Healthcare System.

The anticipated total contract value, including options, is $3,850,000, which falls below the $4.5 million threshold for non-manufacturing NAICS codes as required by FAR 19.1306(a)(2). The applicable NAICS code is 811210 – Electronic and Precision Equipment Repair and Maintenance, with a size standard of $34.0 million.

MedServ Solutions, Inc. has previously performed similar work under Contract No. 36C24420C0087 and received satisfactory to exceptional performance ratings in the Contractor Performance Assessment Reporting System (CPARS). The firm has been determined to be a responsible contractor. The requirement is not currently being performed by an 8(a) participant under FAR Subpart 19.8.

C.4 VAAR 852.219-73 VA NOTICE OF TOTAL SET-ASIDE FOR CERTIFIED SERVICE-DISABLED VETERAN-OWNED SMALL BUSINESSES (JAN 2023) (DEVIATION)

(a) Definition. for the Department of Veterans Affairs, ''Service-disabled Veteran-owned small business concern or SDVOSB'':
(1) Means a small business concern—
(i) Not less than 51 percent of which is owned by one or more service-disabled Veterans or, in the case of any publicly owned business, not less than 51 percent of the stock of which is owned by one or more service-disabled Veterans or eligible surviving spouses...
(b) General. In order for a concern to submit an offer and be eligible for the award of an SDVOSB set-aside or sole source contract, the concern must qualify as a small business concern under the size standard corresponding to the NAICS code assigned to the contract and be listed as an SDVOSB participant in the SBA certification database as set forth in 13 CFR 128.
(1) Offers received from entities that are not certified SDVOSBs and listed in the SBA certification database at the time of offer shall not be considered.
(2) Any award resulting from this solicitation shall be made to a certified SDVOSB listed in the SBA certification database who is eligible at the time of submission of offer(s) and at the time of award.

E.9 52.212-2 EVALUATION—COMMERCIAL PRODUCTS AND COMMERCIAL SERVICES (NOV 2021)

(a) The Government will award a contract resulting from this solicitation to the responsible offeror whose offer conforming to the solicitation will be most advantageous to the Government, price and other factors considered. The following factors shall be used to evaluate offers:

Quotes will be evaluated on the following factors:
1. Technical Approach
2. Experience
3. Past Performance

Each factor will be evaluated and given a rating of Acceptable or Unacceptable. The Government will evaluate quotations using the comparative evaluation process where quotations will be compared to one another to determine which provides the best benefit to the Government. The Government reserves the right to consider a quotation other than the lowest price that provides additional benefit(s).

(b) Options. The Government will evaluate offers for award purposes by adding the total price for all options to the total price for the basic requirement. The Government may determine that an offer is unacceptable if the option prices are significantly unbalanced. Evaluation of options shall not obligate the Government to exercise the option(s).

FACTOR 1: CHALLENGE SCENARIO
Offerors shall provide a (1) proposed Deployment Approach (A003) (Volume 1: Factor 1 – Challenge Scenario 1a) as well as a (2) proposed Deployment Approach (A003) (Volume 1: Factor 1 – Challenge Scenario 1b) based on both of the following scenarios.

The Program Executive Office Defense Healthcare Management Systems (PEO DHMS) is modernizing its global healthcare capabilities, which includes the multi-faceted deployment of DHMS products (Federal Electronic Health Record (EHR) and Operational Medicine (OpMed)). The objective is to create a secure, seamless, and interoperable health information ecosystem that supports warfighters from the point of injury in austere, forward-deployed locations to definitive care.

Offerors shall propose an innovative and scalable deployment plan addressing two interconnected lines of effort as outlined below. The proposed approach shall address hardware and/or component installation and configuration across various networks, continuous patient data access, and comprehensive user training tailored to the operational environment and a diverse user audience. Additionally, the plan shall incorporate proactive risk mitigation strategies to address deployment complexities and ensure mission success.

1a) Federal EHR product modernization and deployment: This effort focuses on the full implementation of the Federal EHR at established, high-capacity treatment facilities.

Scope:
• One large accredited Outside Continental U.S. (OCONUS) medical center (250 beds), including two community hospitals (125 beds) and five associated out-patient clinics spread across multiple time zones with approximately 5,000 total users that includes foreign national employees and 50,000 beneficiaries.

The IMS shall be developed and maintained in accordance with generally accepted schedule management practices, including Government Accountability Office (GAO) Schedule Assessment Guide best practices and Defense Contract Management Agency (DCMA) schedule quality guidance. The Government may assess the IMS using recognized schedule quality metrics to evaluate logic integrity, critical path validity, and overall schedule health. If the Government determines that the IMS does not meet contractual or quality requirements, the contractor shall correct identified deficiencies and resubmit the IMS within a timeframe specified by the Government, at no additional cost. IMS submissions that are not logic-driven, contain excessive constraints or date overrides, lack a valid critical path, or otherwise fail to support Government schedule integration and oversight may be rejected by the Government. Acceptance of the IMS does not relieve the Contractor of responsibility for maintaining schedule accuracy and realism.

M.2 Evaluation of Factors
• Factor 1: Technical
o Subfactor 1.1: Written Technical Approach: The Government will evaluate the offeror's written proposal to assess the degree to which it demonstrates a clear understanding of the PWS requirements and presents a sound, feasible, and comprehensive approach. For Track 2 offerors, this evaluation will be limited to the PWS sections they have proposed.
o Subfactor 1.2: Oral Presentation: For offerors in the competitive range, the Government will evaluate the Key Personnel's performance during the scenario-based challenges. The evaluation will assess:
▪ The team's ability to quickly comprehend complex problems.
▪ The quality, creativity, and viability of the proposed solution.
▪ The team's collaborative process and communication skills.
▪ The demonstrated expertise of the Key Personnel.
▪ Effective use of any Government-provided tools (such as USAi) to enhance their solution. The results of the Oral Presentation will be used to validate or revise the initial rating of the Written Technical Approach, resulting in a single, consolidated rating for the Technical factor where innovation, commitment to extreme ownership, and effective use of artificial intelligence, are plus factors.

L.4 Phase II - Oral Presentations (Flyoff)
After evaluation of the written proposals, the Government will establish a competitive range in accordance with FAR 15.306(c). Offerors included in the competitive range will be invited to participate in an Oral Presentation, or "flyoff."
• Purpose: The Oral Presentation is intended to validate the offeror's proposed technical approach and allow the Government to assess the problem-solving and communication skills of the proposed Key Personnel.
• Participants: The presentation must be led by the proposed Key Personnel identified in Volume I.
• Format: During the session, the Government will present the offeror's team with one or more scenario-based challenges directly related to the PWS. The team will be given a defined period to collaborate and develop a solution, which they will then present to the Government evaluation panel.

M.3 Award Decision
The Government will make awards to the offerors representing the best value within each track.
• Track 1 (Unrestricted): The Government will rank the proposals based on the evaluation factors. The Source Selection Authority (SSA) will select the top-ranked proposals that represent the best value to the Government.
• Track 2 (Small Business Set-Aside): The Government will separately rank the proposals within this track. The SSA will select the top-ranked proposals that represent the best value to the Government.

M.1 Basis for Award
This is a competitive best value tradeoff source selection. The evaluation process will be conducted in two phases. Phase I will consist of the evaluation of the written proposals. Phase II will consist of Oral Presentations for those offerors determined to be in the competitive range. The final evaluation will incorporate the results of both phases.
Factor 1 (Technical) is significantly more important than Factor 2 (Past Performance). Factors 1 and 2, when combined, are significantly more important than Factor 3 (Cost/Price). As the non-cost/price factors become more equal among offerors, the importance of Cost/Price will increase.

Visual Innovation Platform (VIP) and Product Lifecycle Management (PLM):

SECTION M – EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD

M.1 Basis for Award
The Government intends to award a single contract to the responsible offeror whose quotation is determined to be Technically Acceptable and offers the Lowest Price. The Government will evaluate quotations and may make award without discussions. Therefore, the offeror's initial quotation should contain its best terms.

M.2 Evaluation Approach
The Government will use a Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process. Technical quotations will be evaluated on a pass/fail basis. Price will not be evaluated for tradeoff against technical factors.

M.3 Technical Acceptability
To be rated Technically Acceptable, the quotation must clearly demonstrate compliance with all requirements of the solicitation, including:
-Relevant experience performing similar facility condition assessments
-Qualified personnel, including a licensed Professional Engineer (PE)
-Technical approach that meets all Statement of Work requirements
-Ability to meet the required schedule
Failure to meet any of the above criteria will result in a rating of Technically Unacceptable, and the quotation will not be considered for award.

M.4 Price Evaluation
Price will be evaluated for completeness and reasonableness in accordance with FAR 13.106-3.

C.3.8.4 Software Development and Technical Labor Support

The contractor shall provide Software Development and Technical Labor Support for the NextGen Passport Personalization Printers within the Non-Production Environment (NPE), passport operations, and support for CA service desk tickets. 

The contractor shall: 

Provide support for the Print Controller software to successfully communicate with the TDIS.

This application will have an easy-to-use graphical user interface (GUI) and can run on a separate PC or laptop that can be connected directly to the personalization printers. The application will be able to accept the passport print jobs in XML format, perform chip and coding, engrave on the personalization page, and print the endorsement page.

Develop comprehensive test cases and test scenarios, and perform end to end integration, usability and performance testing working with the support site location team to ensure robustness of software, and highest quality and accurate production of passport books.

Assist in supporting GitLab updates, logging defects in ELM, and work with CA Security in scanning and remediation, and enhancing any user level documentation.

C.3.7.2 Incident Management (IcM) – Service Desk Procedures

The Contractor shall provide the Government access to a Service Desk. The Service Desk shall be responsive to the Government 24 hours a day, seven days a week. The Contractor shall document, develop, and follow Service Desk procedures using Incident Management/ Service Desk approaches as discussed in Information Technology Infrastructure Library (ITIL). The Service Desk shall act as the central point of contact between Contractor's service providers and the Government users on a day-to-day basis. It is also a focal point for incident reports and for service requests. ITIL 2011 defines an incident as: An unplanned interruption to an IT Service or reduction in the quality of an IT service. Failure of a configuration item that has not yet affected service is also an incident — for example, failure of a module in the printer that has redundant capability such as the failure of a single laser module in a two-laser module printer. ITIL Incident Management manages the lifecycle of all Incidents. The primary objective of Incident Management is to manage the return of service to users as quickly as possible.

A Service Request is a user request for information, advice, or a standard change (a pre-approved change that is low risk, relatively common and follows a procedure e.g. a password reset (ITIL v3)).

The Incident Management process shall start within one hour of the time the Government contacts the Contractor Service Desk. Within four hours from the Government notification, the Contractor shall document and implement a mitigation strategy to remedy the issue. However, in cases where an Issuance Site does not have a serviceable personalization printer the service desk must ensure that actions are taken that return the site to operation within four hours.

SECTION M – EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD

M.1 Basis for Award
The Government intends to award a single contract to the responsible offeror whose quotation is determined to be Technically Acceptable and offers the Lowest Price. The Government will evaluate quotations and may make award without discussions. Therefore, the offeror's initial quotation should contain its best terms.

M.2 Evaluation Approach
The Government will use a Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process. Technical quotations will be evaluated on a pass/fail basis. Price will not be evaluated for tradeoff against technical factors.

M.3 Technical Acceptability
To be rated Technically Acceptable, the quotation must clearly demonstrate compliance with all requirements of the solicitation, including:
- Relevant experience performing similar facility condition assessments
- Qualified personnel, including a licensed Professional Engineer (PE)
- Technical approach that meets all Statement of Work requirements
- Ability to meet the required schedule
Failure to meet any of the above criteria will result in a rating of Technically Unacceptable, and the quotation will not be considered for award.

M.4 Price Evaluation
Price will be evaluated for completeness and reasonableness in accordance with FAR 13.106-3.

PURPOSE: This SOP establishes guidelines for USAREUR-AF G4 Logistics Automation Division (LAD) Staging Facility operations and the supported customers for issuing and receiving of Tactical Enterprise Logistics Systems (TELS), Logistics Information Systems (LIS), and Commercial off the Shelf Information Technology (COTS IT) equipment for "UNCLASSIFIED" PM fielded U.S. Army Government property and commercial vendors. The USAREUR-AF G4 LAD Staging Facility, located in Miesau, Germany, is the centralized point in Europe for Army Program Managers dealing with TELS, LIS, COTS IT, Life Cycle Replacement (LCR), and shortages fielded to units within the USAREUR-AF Theater of Operations. Equipment is fielded and issued based on the unit's MTOE, TDA, and ATOE authorizations. This facility utilizes Global Combat Support System- Army (GCSS-Army) Property Book, the Army's supply system of record for accountability of transactions between gaining and losing UICs for TELS, LIS, and COTS IT. This ensures an accurate, auditable trail IAW DoD and HQ DAG4 policy. The USAREUR-AF G4 and G6 TELS, LIS, COTS IT system and program managers maintain fully mission capable (FMC) equipment to fill shortages and or mission requirements.

As there are no authorized resellers, this will be a sole source to ICAN. ICAN, Inc. is the only source capable of providing the required products and services, as there are no authorized resellers for ICAN systems or software. The ICAN system operates on proprietary software, developed and owned by ICAN, Inc. This contract requires ongoing software maintenance and upgrades, which requires access to the system's source code for its core components, the "Data Switch" and "Maestro" applications. Without these updates, the software may not function as intended, potentially resulting in lost data, system outages, and other service interruptions. The Government does not possess the intellectual property rights for this software. As a result, it is legally impossible to provide the source code to any other contractor, precluding third-party maintenance or upgrades. Market Research, as documented in Section 8, confirmed this limitation. Four (4) companies expressed interest in performing the work; however, all were found technically unacceptable solely because they could not obtain access to the necessary proprietary code. ICAN, Inc. has not authorized any resellers to provide their software. Alternative software solutions cannot be considered at this time due to specific requirements for data handling, such as National Marine Electronics Association (NMEA) protocols, real-time testing, and backend data parsing and archival system requirements.

3.0 Price Evaluation
The Government reserves the right to award a contract to an offeror other than the offeror proposing the lowest price when the Government's evaluation determines a proposal is significantly superior from a non-price standpoint and warrants payment by the Government of a premium.
Price is defined as the Offeror's price proposal. The Contracting Officer shall evaluate price independently. Pursuant to FAR 17.202(a), the Government's evaluation of offers shall be inclusive of options. Price proposal in Schedule of Items (Section B - Commodity or Services Schedule on the SF 33) shall be evaluated on the base period and options periods in total, including the Option to Extend Services FAR clauses 52.217-8. The line items will be weighted as follows: Entertainment 39%, Phone – 37%, E-mail – 16%, Video call 7%. Any contract line item identified as ancillary/optional shall not be included in the price evaluation.
Risk Assessment
The Government will conduct a proposal risk assessment. The proposal risk assessment reflects the Government's degree of confidence in the offeror's ability to perform the effort described in their Technical proposal. Proposal risk considers if any aspect of the proposed Technical solution could pose potential adverse impacts on price, schedule or performance of the effort.
Award Without Discussions Offerors are advised an award may be made without discussions (see provision 52.215-1, Instructions to Offerors Competitive Acquisition). The Contracting Officer cannot overemphasize the necessity for the initial proposal of an offeror to provide the Government with sufficient information identifying the offeror's best terms from a cost or price and technical/management standpoint.

36. What are the access requirements at facilities?
Response: Contractor employees working at a BOP facility will be subject to background investigation after award of the contract. Tools brought into the institution's secure perimeter will be inventoried at the beginning and end of each workday by FBOP staff.
